items.title: [BUG] [v1.1.0] Native Open Folder dialog opens clipped/off-screen from welcome Open File action
items.body:
Title: [BUG] [v1.1.0] Native Open Folder dialog opens clipped/off-screen from welcome screen Open File action
Severity: Medium

## Reproduction Steps

1. Launch Cortex IDE to the welcome screen (no workspace open).
2. Ensure the main Cortex window is visible and focused.
3. Click the top-bar `Open File` button.
4. Observe the native `Open Folder` dialog placement and visible bounds.

## Expected Result

The native file/folder chooser should be fully visible within display bounds (or centered), with complete controls and no clipped edges.

## Actual Result

The `Open Folder` native dialog appears offset and partially off-screen/clipped. The right side and/or lower area is truncated, reducing usable controls and making file navigation unreliable.

## Video Evidence

- File: `bounty-submissions/artifacts/cortex-open-folder-clipped-repro-final.mp4`
- Inline URL: `https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3d6102a7-86ec-4086-980b-34696b4916a7`
- Bug Timestamp: `00:05 - 00:08` (dialog visible and clipped)
- Integrity Hash (SHA256): `4ddcdc08e841c5f1ec683940b48ab8a68629e7057d0a15969e27dfa9b574cd4d`
- Metadata: `72 frames`, `8 FPS`, `1706x1018` (~9.0s)

## Security Impact

This is a native UI boundary/placement failure that can degrade trusted workspace/file-selection flows. In practical terms:

- Users can be prevented from safely reviewing full picker context before selecting directories.
- Truncated dialog UI increases risk of accidental path selection and unintended workspace scope.
- The onboarding/open-workspace flow becomes unreliable under affected desktop geometry conditions, impacting availability and correctness.

## Environment

- Cortex Version: `1.1.0`
- OS: Linux `6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2` (WSLg desktop host)
- Shell: `bash`
- Configuration: welcome screen state (no active project)
- Fresh Profile: Yes (clean launch state used during capture sequence)

## Proof of Concept Timeline

- `00:00` Cursor marker enters Cortex window and approaches top-bar actions.
- `00:03` Pointer moves to `Open File`.
- `00:04` Click action is performed.
- `00:05` Native `Open Folder` dialog appears clipped/off-screen.
- `00:05 - 00:08` Clipped state persists.

## Validation Checklist

- [x] Native Cortex IDE UI is visible.
- [x] Real pointer movement is visible (yellow cursor marker).
- [x] Timeline is continuous in the capture.
- [x] Steps match the captured interaction.
- [x] Expected vs actual behavior is explicit.
- [x] Supporting screenshots confirm the same clipped state.

## Supporting Evidence

- `bounty-submissions/artifacts/repro1_step2_dialog_retry.png`
- `bounty-submissions/artifacts/cortex_open_folder_dialog_visible.png`
- `bounty-submissions/artifacts/repro-seq1/seq_004.png`

items.title: Question: Why were my PRs closed without merging?
items.body:
Hi maintainers,

I submitted several PRs for the bounty program but they were closed without merging:

- #45: Network Stack (Bounty $140)
- #48: Notifications Stack
- #49: Media Stack
- #50: Observability Stack
- #52: Notifications Stack

These PRs all showed "mergeable: true" and had substantial code changes (500-2000+ lines). I would like to understand why they were closed.

Is there something I can do to get them merged? Or was there an issue with the bounty system?

Thanks!

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] IDE mode gets stuck on a loading spinner after clicking New File

labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: This issue or pull request already exists

items.body: ### Project Cortex IDE ### Description After I click `New File` on the welcome screen and switch to `IDE` mode, the left Explorer sidebar finishes loading, but the main area does not. It stays on a loading spinner instead of showing the normal IDE workspace. ### Error Message No error message was shown in the UI. ### Debug Logs None. ### System Information Windows 11 Alpha desktop build ### Screenshots Before switching to IDE mode: After switching to IDE mode: ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Open Cortex IDE. 2. Click `New File` on the welcome screen. 3. Click `IDE` in the top-left mode switch. 4. Wait a few seconds. ### Expected Behavior IDE mode should open the main workspace normally. ### Actual Behavior The Explorer sidebar shows `No folder opened`, but the main area stays on a loading spinner. ### Additional Context This looks like the sidebar finishes loading, but the main IDE area gets stuck in a loading state.

items.title: [BOUNTY] 🔴 Red Team: Block Explorer & Dashboard Hardening (75 RTC)
items.body:
## Red Team Bounty: Block Explorer Security

**Reward: 75 RTC** (~$7.50 at reference rate)

### Target
The Block Explorer and Hardware Museum merged in Rustchain PR #4 by @erdogan98.

**Code location:** `explorer/` directory (index.html, museum.html — 1022 lines)

### What to Attack

1. **Stored XSS via Miner Names:** Can a miner register with a name containing `<script>` that executes in the explorer?
2. **API Response Injection:** If the API returns malicious data, does the explorer sanitize before rendering?
3. **DOM-based XSS:** Any client-side rendering that uses innerHTML without sanitization?
4. **Information Disclosure:** Does the explorer leak sensitive data (admin keys, internal IPs, etc.)?
5. **Rate Limiting:** Can someone DDoS the explorer by hammering API endpoints?
6. **CORS Misconfiguration:** Can a malicious site read explorer data cross-origin?

### Deliverables
- Security report with severity ratings
- PoC for each finding
- Patched HTML files (bonus RTC)

### Claiming
Comment below.

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] CortexSourceControl component exported from barrel index but never imported or rendered — dead code in bundle
items.body: ### Project ide ### Description `CortexSourceControl` (src/components/cortex/CortexSourceControl.tsx) is exported from the cortex barrel `index.ts` but is never imported or rendered by any component in the application. The Source Control sidebar panel actually renders `CortexGitPanel` (lazy-imported in `CortexSidebarContainer.tsx` line 7), not `CortexSourceControl`. The component is a ~245-line source control view that ships in the production bundle but is dead code — it was superseded by `CortexGitPanel` and never wired into the layout. **Evidence chain:** 1. `CortexSourceControl` is exported from `src/components/cortex/index.ts` line 152 2. `CortexSidebarContainer.tsx` line 7 lazy-imports `CortexGitPanel` and renders it at line 117-118 for the `"git"` sidebar tab 3. No file in `src/` (excluding tests) imports `CortexSourceControl` ### Error Message ```shell N/A — silent dead code, no runtime error ``` ### Debug Logs ```shell # Grep confirms zero non-test imports of CortexSourceControl: $ grep -rn "import.*CortexSourceControl" src/ --include="*.tsx" --include="*.ts" | grep -v test | grep -v index.ts # (no results) # The sidebar renders CortexGitPanel instead: # src/components/cortex/layout/CortexSidebarContainer.tsx:7 const CortexGitPanel = lazy(() => import("@/components/cortex/CortexGitPanel").then(m => ({ default: m.CortexGitPanel }))); # src/components/cortex/layout/CortexSidebarContainer.tsx:116-118 Loading...}> ``` ### System Information ```shell Cortex IDE: alpha OS: Microsoft Windows 11 Home Single Language Component: src/components/cortex/CortexSourceControl.tsx (111-245), exported at src/components/cortex/index.ts:152 ``` ### Screenshots ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Open Cortex IDE and switch to IDE mode. 2. Click the "Source Control" button in the activity bar. 3. Observe the sidebar renders `CortexGitPanel` (commit message input, Changes/Staged Changes/Stashes sections, branch selector at bottom). 4. Search the codebase: `grep -rn "import.*CortexSourceControl" src/ --include="*.tsx" --include="*.ts" | grep -v test | grep -v index.ts` — returns zero results. ### Expected Behavior Components exported from barrel index files should be imported and rendered by at least one consumer. Dead exports should be removed to reduce bundle size and avoid developer confusion. ### Actual Behavior `CortexSourceControl` is exported from `src/components/cortex/index.ts` (line 152) but is never imported or rendered by any component. The Source Control sidebar uses `CortexGitPanel` instead. The 245-line component contributes dead code to the production bundle.

items.title: [BOUNTY: 40-100 RTC] RustChain Wallet Browser Extension + MetaMask Snap
items.body:
## RustChain Wallet Browser Extension — Chrome + Firefox

### The Bounty

Build a browser-based RTC wallet extension for Chrome and Firefox, based on our existing [wallet crypto module](https://github.com/Scottcjn/rustchain-wallet) (Ed25519, BIP39 seed phrases, AES-256-GCM keystores).

### Payout

| Milestone | Reward |
|-----------|--------|
| Working Chrome extension with core wallet features | **40 RTC** |
| Published on Chrome Web Store | **+10 RTC** |
| Firefox Add-on version (addons.mozilla.org) | **+10 RTC** |
| MetaMask Snap integration (see below) | **+40 RTC** |

**Total possible: 100 RTC**

### Core Features (Required for 40 RTC)

1. **Create wallet** — generate BIP39 24-word seed phrase, derive Ed25519 keypair
2. **Import wallet** — restore from seed phrase or private key
3. **View balance** — query RustChain node for RTC balance
4. **Send RTC** — sign and submit transfers via `/wallet/transfer/signed` endpoint
5. **Transaction history** — show recent sends/receives from ledger
6. **Encrypted storage** — keystore encrypted with user password (AES-256-GCM), never stored in plaintext
7. **Multiple wallets** — manage more than one RTC address
8. **Network selector** — point to mainnet (50.28.86.131) or custom node URL

### Existing Code to Build On

The crypto module is already built and tested:

```python
# rustchain_crypto.py — Port this to JavaScript/TypeScript
- BIP39 24-word mnemonic generation
- Ed25519 signing (via PyNaCl/libsodium)
- PBKDF2 key derivation (100,000 iterations)
- AES-256-GCM encrypted keystores
- Address format: RTC + SHA256(pubkey)[:40]
```

**JS equivalents:**
- BIP39: `bip39` npm package
- Ed25519: `tweetnacl` or `@noble/ed25519`
- AES-256-GCM: Web Crypto API (built into browsers)
- PBKDF2: Web Crypto API

The server-side signed transfer endpoint already exists:

```
POST https://50.28.86.131/wallet/transfer/signed
{
  "from_address": "RTCa1b2c3d4...",
  "to_address": "RTC9876543210...",
  "amount_rtc": 100.5,
  "memo": "Payment",
  "nonce": 1733420000000,
  "signature": "hex_ed25519_sig",
  "public_key": "hex_pubkey"
}
```

### MetaMask Snap Integration (Bonus — 40 RTC)

[MetaMask Snaps](https://metamask.io/snaps/) allow non-EVM chains to integrate with MetaMask. This would let RustChain users manage RTC directly from MetaMask.

**What a Snap would do:**
- Custom RTC account type inside MetaMask
- Send/receive RTC through MetaMask UI
- Sign RustChain transactions with MetaMask-managed keys
- Show RTC balance alongside ETH/ERC-20 tokens

**Snap requirements:**
- Uses MetaMask Snaps SDK (`@metamask/snaps-sdk`)
- Implements `onRpcRequest` for RustChain JSON-RPC calls
- Custom UI via `snap_dialog` for transaction confirmation
- Published to MetaMask Snap registry

This is harder than the standalone extension but would be huge — every MetaMask user could instantly hold RTC.

### Alternative: Other Open Wallet Integrations

If MetaMask Snap is too complex, other options worth exploring:

| Wallet | Integration Path | Difficulty |
|--------|-----------------|------------|
| **MetaMask Snap** | Snaps SDK, custom account type | Hard |
| **Rabby Wallet** | Similar to MetaMask Snap architecture | Hard |
| **WalletConnect** | WC protocol adapter for RustChain | Medium |
| **Keplr** | Custom chain config (Cosmos-style) | Medium |
| **Standalone extension** | Our own Chrome/Firefox extension | Easiest |

Start with the standalone extension. If you want to tackle MetaMask Snap too, that's the bonus 40 RTC.

### Technical Requirements

1. **Manifest V3** (Chrome) / WebExtensions API (Firefox)
2. **No external key servers** — all crypto happens locally in the browser
3. **Seed phrase NEVER leaves the extension** — no telemetry, no analytics, no third-party calls except to RustChain node
4. **Open source** on GitHub
5. **Clean, minimal UI** — balance, send, receive, settings
6. **Web Crypto API preferred** over bundled crypto libraries where possible

### Security Requirements (Non-Negotiable)

- Private keys encrypted at rest with user password
- Auto-lock after 5 minutes of inactivity
- Seed phrase shown only once during creation (with confirmation step)
- Transaction signing requires password/unlock
- No eval(), no remote code loading, no CDN dependencies
- CSP headers in manifest

### How to Claim

1. Build the extension and publish source on GitHub
2. Comment here with:
   - GitHub repo link
   - Chrome Web Store / Firefox Add-on link (once approved)
   - Demo video or screenshots
   - Your RTC wallet address

### Why This Matters

Right now sending RTC requires either the desktop GUI wallet or curl commands. A browser extension:
- **Lowers the barrier** — anyone with Chrome can hold and send RTC
- **Enables web integration** — dApps, BoTTube tipping, bounty claims could connect to the wallet
- **MetaMask Snap** would put RTC in front of 30M+ MetaMask users
- **Legitimacy** — every real chain has a browser wallet

### Resources

- Wallet crypto module: Built on Ed25519 + BIP39 + AES-256-GCM
- Signed transfer API: `POST /wallet/transfer/signed` on any RustChain node
- Balance API: `GET /wallet/balance?miner_id={wallet}`
- MetaMask Snaps: https://docs.metamask.io/snaps/
- Web Crypto API: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Web_Crypto_API

**Wallet:** Include your wallet address when claiming.

6d9d3ef

Encountered a bug where the cargo bounty board couldn't be opened, when I debugged it, it was because the station AI had skipped a bounty and their name had text that markup tried to parse but couldn't throwing an exception.

Someone needs to go through all useages of SetMarkup and see if they should be changed to SetMarkupPermissive or not depending on the inputs.

items.body:
**Expensify/Expensify Issue URL:** https://github.com/Expensify/Expensify/issues/603735 **Slack conversation:** https://expensify.slack.com/archives/C03U7DCU4/p1771944902661119?thread_ts=1771944902.661119&cid=C03U7DCU4 --- ## Action Performed 1. Open a report that has multiple expenses, where at least one expense has an RBR indicator (violation, hold, field error, receipt error, etc.) 2. Look at the expense carousel in the report preview ## Expected Result The expense that requires action (has RBR) should appear **first** in the carousel so the user can immediately identify and act on it. ## Actual Result Expenses appear in backend insertion order. RBR-flagged expenses can be buried at position 5+ in the carousel, making them easy to miss entirely — especially since only the first 11 are shown. ## Root Cause There is no RBR-aware sorting anywhere in the transaction display path: 1. **Carousel** — [`MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent.tsx:~506`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/components/ReportActionItem/MoneyRequestReportPreview/MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent.tsx#L506) does `transactions.slice(0, 11)` with no sort beforehand. 2. **Report transaction list** — [`MoneyRequestReportTransactionList.tsx:~325`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/components/MoneyRequestReportView/MoneyRequestReportTransactionList.tsx#L325) sorts by date/column only; `visualOrderTransactionIDs` (which drives RHP carousel navigation) inherits this non-RBR-aware order. The data flows from Onyx derived store → `Object.values()` (insertion order) → carousel/list — no step prioritizes actionable items. ## Proposed Fix Sort RBR-flagged transactions to the front in both locations: - **Carousel:** Sort `transactions` by RBR status before the `.slice(0, 11)` in `MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent`. - **Transaction list:** Use RBR status as a primary sort key in `MoneyRequestReportTransactionList`, with the existing date/column sort as secondary. ### Existing infrastructure to reuse - [`shouldShowRBR`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/libs/TransactionPreviewUtils.ts#L411-L425) from `TransactionPreviewUtils.createTransactionPreviewConditionals()` - `filteredViolations` already computed per-transaction in `MoneyRequestReportTransactionList` (~lines 216-237) - Report-level RBR prioritization pattern in [`SidebarUtils.ts:~533`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/libs/SidebarUtils.ts#L533-L535) ## Open Questions - Should RBR-first sorting override a user-selected column sort (date, merchant, etc.) in the report view, or only apply as the default? - Should sorting be centralized in `useReportWithTransactionsAndViolations` so all consumers benefit? ## Platforms - [x] Android: App - [x] Android: mWeb Chrome - [x] iOS: App - [x] iOS: mWeb Safari - [x] iOS: mWeb Chrome - [x] Windows: Chrome - [x] MacOS: Chrome / Safari
Issue OwnerCurrent Issue Owner: @grgia

  • Upwork Job ID: 2033929183187241566

```bash
# 为每个服务创建独立 database + user
create_db "nextcloud" "${NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD}"
create_db "gitea"     "${GITEA_DB_PASSWORD}"
create_db "outline"   "${OUTLINE_DB_PASSWORD}"
create_db "authentik" "${AUTHENTIK_DB_PASSWORD}"
create_db "grafana"   "${GRAFANA_DB_PASSWORD}"
```

脚本必须是**幂等的**（重复执行不报错，不重置已有数据）。

### 2. Redis 多数据库分配

在各服务的 compose 中通过 `?db=N` 参数隔离：

```
DB 0 — Authentik
DB 1 — Outline
DB 2 — Gitea
DB 3 — Nextcloud
DB 4 — Grafana sessions
```

### 3. 备份集成

`scripts/backup-databases.sh`：
- `pg_dumpall` 备份所有 PostgreSQL 数据库
- `redis-cli BGSAVE` 触发 Redis 持久化
- 压缩为 `.tar.gz`，保留最近 7 天
- 可选：上传到 MinIO

### 4. 健康检查

所有数据库容器必须有严格的健康检查，其他 Stack 通过 `depends_on: condition: service_healthy` 等待。

### 5. 网络隔离

数据库服务**不加入** `proxy` 网络，仅暴露给 `internal` 网络，不通过 Traefik 对外暴露（管理界面除外）。

### 6. 环境变量

```bash
POSTGRES_ROOT_PASSWORD=
REDIS_PASSWORD=
MARIADB_ROOT_PASSWORD=
PGADMIN_EMAIL=
PGADMIN_PASSWORD=
# 各服务 DB 密码
NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD=
GITEA_DB_PASSWORD=
OUTLINE_DB_PASSWORD=
AUTHENTIK_DB_PASSWORD=
```

## 验收标准

- [ ] `init-databases.sh` 运行后所有数据库和用户创建成功
- [ ] `init-databases.sh` 重复运行不报错
- [ ] pgAdmin 可访问并连接 PostgreSQL
- [ ] 其他 Stack (Gitea/Nextcloud) 可通过内部 hostname 连接数据库
- [ ] 数据库容器**不**暴露到宿主机端口（仅内部网络）
- [ ] `backup-databases.sh` 生成有效的 `.tar.gz` 备份
- [ ] README 包含各服务连接字符串示例

## 认领方式

评论 "我来认领"。

items.title: [BOUNTY PROPOSAL] IoT-Integrated Hydropower MRV with Device-Level DIDs and Automated IoT Verification (Verra ACM0002 v22.0)
items.body:
## Status Request

**I submitted PRs [#5687](https://github.com/hashgraph/guardian/pull/5687)  and
 [#5715](https://github.com/hashgraph/guardian/pull/5715)  for the DLT Earth Methodology
Bounty Program approximately **1 month ago** and have not received any feedback or
bounty status update. I am creating this formal proposal issue as required by the
contribution process and respectfully requesting a review.**

I would be highly obliged if a maintainer could provide information on:
- Whether this submission qualifies for the bounty
- Whether any changes are required to the policy or documentation
- Whether the folder needs to be moved to the main methodology library
  (I do not have write access to merge files into the main Guardian repo directly)

---

## Summary

This is a formal bounty proposal for an IoT-integrated, DLT-native Hydropower MRV policy
implementing **Verra ACM0002 v22.0** (Grid-connected electricity generation from renewable
sources) on the Hedera Guardian platform.

I have completely overhauled this submission to address the **"trust gap"** in traditional
MRV. This is not a simple copy of the CDM ACM0002 template — it is a **Technical Evolution**
that introduces Device-Level DIDs for turbines and Automated IoT Telemetry (Flow, Head, pH).
This contribution digitizes the verification layer, moving from manual reporting to
cryptographically signed machine-level proof — a feature currently missing from the
Methodology Library.

>  **Repository:** https://github.com/BikramBiswas786/guardian/tree/main/methodologies/hydropower-mrv
>  **Policy File:** https://github.com/BikramBiswas786/guardian/blob/main/methodologies/hydropower-mrv/Hydropower%20MRV_ACM0002.policy
>  **Related PRs:** #5687 and #5715

---

## Problem: The Trust Gap in Traditional MRV

The existing Guardian library contains a generic **CDM ACM0002** template that suffers from
three critical weaknesses:

1. **Manual Data Entry** — Operators manually input energy data, which is prone to manipulation.
2. **Project-Level Reporting** — Data is aggregated at the project level, hiding individual
   turbine performance.
3. **Delayed Verification** — Verification occurs months after generation, making fraud hard
   to detect.

---

## Solution: Three DLT-Native Innovations

### 1.  Device-Level DIDs (Machine Identity)
Every turbine is assigned a unique **Decentralized Identifier (DID)** anchored on Hedera.
- All telemetry is **cryptographically signed** by the turbine's private key.
- Guardian verifies the signature before accepting data into the MRV workflow.
- Eliminates "ghost" energy generation and double-counting at the source.

### 2.  Real-Time IoT Telemetry (Proof of Physics)
Instead of tracking only total energy (MWh), this policy tracks the **physics of generation**:

| Parameter   | Unit  | Purpose                                    |
|-------------|-------|--------------------------------------------|
| Flow Rate   | m³/s  | Volumetric water flow through turbines     |
| Head Height | m     | Effective hydraulic head pressure          |
| Turbidity   | NTU   | Water clarity affecting turbine efficiency |
| pH Level    | —     | Environmental compliance monitoring        |

Cross-verification: if reported MWh exceeds the theoretical maximum
(`P = η × ρ × g × Q × H`), the system **automatically flags it for VVB audit**.

### 3.  REC NFTs with Multi-Tier Royalties
- Each NFT = 1 MWh of verified renewable energy (Hedera Token Service).
- NFT metadata links to the turbine's signed **HCS telemetry messages**.
- Built-in royalty mechanism creates sustainable revenue for project developers.

---

## Why This Is NOT a Duplicate of the Existing CDM ACM0002

| Feature          | Existing CDM ACM0002     | This Policy (Verra ACM0002 v22.0)       |
|------------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Standard body    | CDM (UNFCCC)             | Verra ACM0002 v22.0                     |
| Data entry       | Manual                   | Automated IoT M2M                       |
| Identity         | Project-level (1 DID)    | Device-level (1 DID per turbine)        |
| Verification     | Periodic human audit     | Real-time cryptographic telemetry       |
| Fraud detection  | None                     | Physics cross-verification              |
| Asset type       | CER tokens               | REC NFTs with multi-tier royalties      |

---

## Files Submitted

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Hydropower_MRV_ACM0002.policy` | Full Guardian policy export (MGS) |
| `SCHEMA.json` | Turbine-level telemetry schemas |
| `TECHNICAL_WHITEPAPER.md` | Architecture, physics math, and differentiation |
| `BOUNTY_REQUEST.md` | Formal bounty justification |
| `EVIDENCE.md` | Hedera testnet DID topics and REC token config |

---

## Testnet Evidence

- **Operator ID:** `0.0.6255927` (Hedera Testnet)
- **Schema UUID:** `59ad4c29-043a-4d63-ad0a-7d2dfa5d32ca`
- **Token:** Hydropower REC (HTS NFT with royalties)
- Full details in `EVIDENCE.md`

---

## DLT Earth Bounty Alignment

- ✅ Novel DLT application — device DIDs + REC NFTs not present in any existing Guardian methodology
- ✅ Fills a real gap — no hydropower-specific MRV exists in the Guardian library
- ✅ Distinct standard — Verra ACM0002 v22.0, not the existing CDM version
- ✅ On-chain evidence — live testnet deployment with verifiable transactions

---

**Submitted by:** Bikram Biswas ([@BikramBiswas786](https://github.com/BikramBiswas786))
 biswasbikram786@gmail.com

## About La Tanda

La Tanda is a digital cooperative savings platform (ROSCA) for Honduras. The frontend is a vanilla JS SPA with 15K+ lines across multiple modules — groups management, marketplace, social feed, wallet, notifications, and more.

Own frontend feature development and maintenance:

- Strong vanilla JS skills (not framework-dependent)

items.title: [BOUNTY] Server-Side Architecture Cross-Validation
items.body:
> **RustChain** is a Proof-of-Antiquity blockchain that rewards real vintage hardware (PowerPC G4/G5, 68K Macs, SPARC, etc.) with higher mining multipliers than modern machines. The network uses 6 hardware fingerprint checks to prevent VMs and emulators from earning rewards. There are currently 9 active miners and 3 attestation nodes. The native token is **RTC (RustChain Token)**.
>
> **Live Network:** Node health `curl -sk https://50.28.86.131/health` · Active miners `curl -sk https://50.28.86.131/api/miners` · Explorer `https://50.28.86.131/explorer`
>
> **Repo:** [github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain](https://github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain) · **Bounties:** [github.com/Scottcjn/rustchain-bounties](https://github.com/Scottcjn/rustchain-bounties)

---

## Goal
Implement **server-side cross-validation** that verifies a miner's claimed `device_arch` against its fingerprint data. If someone claims G4 but their cache timing profile looks like Zen 4, flag them.

## Background

Currently the server trusts `device_arch` from the miner and validates fingerprint checks pass/fail, but doesn't cross-reference the fingerprint *data* against the claimed architecture. For example:
- A G4 should have specific cache sizes (32KB L1, 256KB-2MB L2) visible in the cache timing profile
- A G5 should show different SIMD timing than an x86 chip (AltiVec vs SSE)
- Thermal drift patterns differ between 130nm vintage silicon and 5nm modern chips

## Scope & Requirements

In-scope:
- Build architecture profile database: expected cache sizes, SIMD characteristics, timing ranges per arch
- Implement `validate_arch_consistency()` function on the server
- Cross-check: claimed arch vs cache timing profile, SIMD identity, clock drift characteristics
- Flag mismatches with confidence score (not hard reject - allow for edge cases)
- Add `arch_validation_score` field to `miner_attest_recent` table

Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Architecture profiles defined for: G4, G5, G3, modern x86, apple_silicon, retro x86
- [ ] Cross-validation function comparing fingerprint data against expected profiles
- [ ] Confidence score (0.0-1.0) for architecture claim validity
- [ ] Miners with score < 0.3 flagged for manual review
- [ ] Unit tests with real fingerprint data from known hardware
- [ ] PR to main [Rustchain repo](https://github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain)

## Payout
**50 RTC**

## Skills Needed
- Python
- Understanding of CPU microarchitecture differences
- Statistical analysis (comparing timing distributions)
- Hardware fingerprinting concepts

## Resources
- Current fingerprint checks: `fingerprint_checks.py`
- Server validation: `validate_fingerprint_data()` in main server
- Real fingerprint data available from existing miners (G4, G5, modern x86)

items.title: [BOUNTY] Collab Bot Duo — Create Two Bots That Interact With Each Other (75 RTC)

## Summary
Create two BoTTube bots with distinct personalities that interact with each other through comments and response videos.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is meant to demonstrate BoTTube's social surface with real creator interaction, not just two isolated channels. A strong pair of bots can show how agent personalities, comments, and follow-on content reinforce each other.

## Scope
- create two separate BoTTube bots with distinct personas
- give both bots custom avatars and bios
- publish at least `8` videos on each bot
- create at least `5` cross-comments across the pair
- create at least `2` response or callback videos where one bot references the other
- keep the bots active and public long enough for review

## Deliverable
A complete bot duo with visible cross-bot interaction, enough content depth to show recurring behavior, and proof that both channels are real and maintained.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] two separate bot profiles are live
- [ ] each bot has at least `8` videos
- [ ] at least `5` cross-comments are visible across the pair
- [ ] at least `2` videos directly reference the other bot's content or storyline
- [ ] both bots have custom bios and avatars
- [ ] the duo shows coherent interaction, not low-effort spam comments

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- links to both bot profiles
- links to the response videos
- screenshots or links showing cross-comments
- a short description of the bot dynamic or story arc
- your RTC wallet or `miner_id`

## Payout
- `75 RTC` for an accepted duo
- pool: `150 RTC`
- max `1` duo per claimant unless maintainers explicitly approve more

## Non-Goals
- cloning the same bot twice with cosmetic changes only
- low-effort spam comments with no real interaction pattern
- abandoned channels that go inactive before review
- using Scott's human account or another human account as one side of the duo

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for proof and payout review. If your work exposes product bugs in comments, profiles, or bot tooling, open a separate issue in `Scottcjn/bottube` and link it back here.

items.title: Red Team: Hardware Fingerprint Replay & Spoofing (100 RTC)
items.body:
## Red Team: Hardware Fingerprint Replay & Spoofing

**Reward: Up to 100 RTC**

### Target
RIP-PoA fingerprint attestation system

### Scope
1. **Fingerprint replay** — Can you record a real machine's fingerprint and replay it from a VM? (50 RTC)
2. **Clock drift spoofing** — Can you fake oscillator variance to pass the clock-skew check? (25 RTC)
3. **Anti-emulation bypass** — Can you hide VM indicators from the 6-check fingerprint suite? (25 RTC)

### Context
RustChain uses 6 hardware fingerprint checks (clock drift, cache timing, SIMD identity, thermal drift, instruction jitter, anti-emulation) to prevent VM farms from gaming Proof-of-Antiquity rewards.

### Rules
- Test against `pip install clawrtc` miner client
- Report with proof-of-concept code
- Include your RTC wallet in the report
- Responsible disclosure only

> Miner source: https://github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain

# Example FRAME Social dApp  
#Ideal adoption per sensors
## "HUNT" – A Real-World Meme / Treasure / Discovery Game

This is a social media dApp designed for **instant adoption**.

It mixes:

- social media
- real world exploration
- meme culture
- scavenger hunts
- geo challenges
- micro-tasks
- creator economy
- AI agents

Think:

Pokémon Go  
+ Reddit  
+ TikTok  
+ scavenger hunts  
+ bounty economy

All running on FRAME.

---

# Core Idea

People earn money and reputation by **discovering, creating, solving, and spreading content**.

Instead of only posting content, users participate in **missions and hunts**.

Examples:

- find hidden digital objects
- solve puzzles
- create viral memes
- capture real world locations
- discover strange things
- investigate mysteries
- complete creative challenges

Everything creates **verifiable receipts** so rewards are fair.

---

# Identity

Users join using their FRAME identity.

Identity includes:

- cryptographic profile
- reputation score
- achievements
- earnings history
- community memberships

Identity is portable.

Your followers and reputation move with you.

---

# The Map

The main interface is a **global map**.

The map shows:

- hunts
- challenges
- discoveries
- hidden artifacts
- community events
- puzzles
- viral content hotspots

Players explore the world digitally and physically.

---

# Hunts

A hunt is a challenge posted by anyone.

Example hunts:

- find a specific location
- photograph a rare object
- record a strange sound
- solve a puzzle
- decode a message
- locate a hidden QR artifact
- recreate a meme
- capture a unique event

Each hunt includes:

- reward
- rules
- verification logic

The first person to complete the hunt gets paid.

---

# Meme Economy

Memes become **competitive challenges**.

Example:

Someone posts:

"Create the funniest version of this meme"

Community votes.

Winner receives rewards.

Because posts are signed, the original creator is always known.

---

# Viral Challenge System

Users can start challenges like:

- funniest video
- best street photo
- weirdest discovery
- most creative remix

People participate and vote.

Rewards come from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community funding

---

# Discovery Mode

Users can post **discoveries**.

Examples:

- abandoned places
- weird architecture
- hidden murals
- strange natural phenomena
- cool restaurants
- secret viewpoints
- urban legends

Discoveries become permanent map objects.

People earn rewards if the discovery becomes popular.

---

# Bounties

Anyone can create a bounty.

Example:

Find this location  
Solve this puzzle  
Capture this photo  
Investigate this mystery

Users race to complete it.

The bounty creator deposits funds into a contract.

The winner receives payment automatically.

---

# AI Generated Hunts

Agents generate hunts automatically.

Examples:

- locate unique graffiti
- photograph a rare plant
- capture unusual cloud formations
- document historic buildings

Agents analyze submissions and verify authenticity.

---

# Verification

FRAME allows hunts to be verified using:

- GPS location
